nativeRange.habitat: Small, shallow forest streams and seasonal pools on the western slopes of the Rakhine Yoma (Arakan) mountains; type locality a stream at most 3 m wide and ~30 cm deep with clear water, slight current, and a bottom of pebbles, rock, and gravel. Waters are monsoon-influenced and seasonally variable.

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Microsorum, mosses) with open midwater swimming lanes
driftwood and leaf litter for natural cover
gentle current from a spray bar or powerhead (4-5x tank-volume turnover)

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous in the wild; in the aquarium readily accepts quality micro-pellets and small flake as a staple. Regular supplementation with small live and frozen foods (Daphnia, Artemia nauplii, micro-bloodworm) improves coloration and conditioning for breeding.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids (rasboras, other small danios)
avoidWith: ornamental long-finned varieties (fin-nipping risk at feeding time)
slow-moving species that cannot compete for food

summary: Small Myanmar danio described 2009 (Kullander & Fang), named for its reticulated black-on-gold snakeskin pattern. Cool-tolerant and beginner-friendly; keep in schools of 8 or more. Often confused in the trade with Zebra Danio and Glowlight Danio. A tight-fitting lid is non-negotiable — this species is a confirmed high-risk jumper.
careNotes:
The Panther Danio is endemic to small, shallow, monsoon-fed forest streams on the western slopes of the Rakhine Yoma in western Myanmar — a range physically separated from the rest of the country by the Arakan mountain chain. It was formally described only in 2009 (Kullander & Fang, Zootaxa) and remains less frequently traded than the superficially similar Zebra Danio (D. rerio) and Glowlight Danio (D. choprae, fw-fish-189), with which it is sometimes confused in the trade. Its distinctive reticulated pattern of dark vertical bars anteriorly and rows of small brown spots posteriorly on a gold base distinguishes it on close inspection.

Water conditions should mirror its cool, clean, well-oxygenated native streams: temperature 18-27 degrees C (the species is notably cool-tolerant compared with most tropical danios), pH 6.8-7.2, and soft water (gH 1-8). Moderate flow (4-5x tank-volume turnover per hour) is recommended; torrent-like current is not required. A tank of at least 60 cm length and 60 L is workable for a small group, but larger footprints allow the natural schooling behaviour to be fully expressed.

Keep in schools of at least 8-10 individuals. Smaller numbers produce skittish behaviour and suppress the dramatic group shoaling displays. Compatible with other small, peaceful species of comparable size; avoid long-finned varieties as fin-nipping has been reported, especially at feeding time.

This species is a high-risk jumper. It can escape through surprisingly small gaps; a very tightly-fitting cover is non-negotiable. Losses to jumping are common when tanks are left even briefly uncovered.
breedingNotes: An egg-scatterer with no parental care, typical of the genus. Condition a group of 8-12 in a well-fed, mixed-sex school; separate a smaller spawning group (e.g., 2-3 females, 3-4 males) into a bare or fine-leaved-plant-lined spawning tank with soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.8-7.0, gH 1-4, 24-26 degrees C). Adults will eat eggs; a mesh or marbles layer over the base protects the spawn. Eggs hatch in approximately 36 hours and fry become free-swimming 3-4 days later. Feed fry infusoria and then Artemia nauplii or micro-crushed flake as they grow. No specialised equipment needed.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Brachydanio aesculapii (Danio aesculapii)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rakhine Yoma, western Myanmar), habitat description, water parameters (temperature 22-27 C, pH 6.9-7.1, hardness 18-90 ppm soft), tank size guidance (60x30 cm minimum), diet (insectivorous; live/frozen supplements), breeding (egg-scatterer, ~36 h incubation, free-swimming at day 4), schooling minimum (8-10), escape-risk warning, and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Danio aesculapii
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes), adult size (max 3.0 cm SL), native range (Rakhine Yoma, south-western Myanmar), habitat (small forest streams, benthopelagic), trophic level 3.0 (consistent with omnivore/insectivore), and IUCN Near Threatened status (2010).

additional.name: Kullander & Fang 2009 — Danio aesculapii, a new species of danio from south-western Myanmar (Zootaxa 2164)
additional.notes: Original species description. Confirms type locality (small stream on western slope of Rakhine Yoma, max 3 m wide, ~30 cm deep, clear water, pebble/rock/gravel substrate), diagnostic colour pattern (6-7 brown vertical bars anteriorly, two rows of small brown spots posteriorly, no D-stripe), and etymology (aesculapii referencing serpentine markings of Aesculapius).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Panther danio, Danio aesculapii
additional.notes: Supplementary trade-hobby source. Confirmed group size recommendation (minimum 6), fin-nipping risk with ornamental long-finned varieties, escape-risk warning (tight-fitting cover essential), and compatible community choices (small cyprinids, tetras, livebearers, rainbowfish, anabantoids, small catfish).

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Danio aesculapii
additional.notes: Wholesale importer trade record. Confirmed country of origin (Myanmar) and traded size (3-4 cm), consistent with FishBase and SeriouslyFish data.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; no parental care. Spawning group conditioned in a planted or bare tank with soft, slightly acidic water. Eggs hatch in ~36 h; fry free-swimming by day 4. A substrate mesh or marbles layer protects eggs from adult predation. No specialist equipment required.
